title: Raman study of layered rock-salt LiCoO2 and its electrochemical lithium deintercalation
abstract:
JOURNAL OF RAMAN SPECTROSCOPY, VOL. 28, 613È617 (1997)
Study of Layered Rock-Salt
and
Raman
LiCoO2
Its Electrochemical Lithium Deintercalation
Minoru Inaba,1* Yasutoshi Iriyama,1 Zempachi Ogumi,1 Yasufumi Todzuka2 and Akimasa Tasaka2
1 Department of Energy and Hydrocarbon Chemistry, Graduate School of Engineering, Kyoto University, Sakyo-ku, Kyoto
606-01, Japan
2 Department of Molecular Science and Technology, Faculty of Engineering, Doshisha University, Tsuzuki-gun, Kyoto
610-03, Japan
Unpolarized and polarized Raman spectra (200–800 cm—1) of
with a layered rock-salt structure were
LiCoO2
measured. The Raman-active lattice modes of
were assigned by polarized Raman measurements of a
LiCoO2
c-axis oriented thin Ðlm. The variation of the Raman spectra of
powder prepared by electrochemical
Li1—xCoO2
lithium deintercalation was investigated, and the spectral changes were well correlated with the structural changes
determined by x-ray di†raction except that peak splitting by the distortion in the monoclinic phase was not
observed. The observed line broadening of the second hexagonal phase and the monoclinic phase indicated that the
lithium ions remaining in the lattice after deintercalation randomly occupy the available sites on the lithium planes
in the lattice the layered rock-salt structure.

ordering on alternating (111) planes of the cubic rock-
salt structure. As a result of this ordering,

study, we prepared a c-axis oriented thin Ðlm of

EXPERIMENTAL
Sample preparation
powder was prepared by a conventional solid-
LiCoO2
phase reaction using
(Wako Pure Chemical)
Li2CO3
and
(Mitsuwa Pure Chemical) as raw materials.
CoCO3
They were mixed and Ðred at 650 ¡C for 12 h and then
kept at 900 ¡C for 24 h under an oxygen gas Ñow. The
powder was used for electrochemical lithium deinterca-
lation measurements.
For polarization measurements, a c-axis oriented thin
Ðlm of
was prepared by PLD on Si(100) sub-
LiCoO2
strates on which Au thin layers (ca. 100 nm) were depos-
ited by d.c. sputtering. A KrF eximer laser (Japan
Storage
Battery,
EXL-200,
248
nm)
was
directed
through a quartz window into a vacuum chamber onto
an LiÈCoÈO target which was to receive a deposit. The
incidence angle between the laser and the target was
45¡. Pressed pellets of oxide powder with an Li/Co
atomic ratio of 1.50 : 1 were sintered at 850 ¡C for 24 h
in oxygen, and were used as the target. The distance
between the target and the substrate was 4.2 cm. Film
deposition was carried out in the presence of 200 mTorr
of oxygen with 1.5 J cm~2 laser pulses at 40 Hz for 30

min. The substrate was heated at 500 ¡C during deposi-
tion. The details of the preparation will be reported
elsewhere.
The powder and thin Ðlm were analyzed by x-ray dif-
fractometry (XRD), (Rigaku, RINT-2500). The XRD
pattern of the Ðlm showed only (00l) lines of LiCoO2 ,
which conÐrmed that the Ðlm was deposited with its
c-axis perpendicular to the substrate surface. The Ðlm
thickness estimated from scanning electron microscopic
observation was about 0.8 lm.
Electrochemical lithium deintercalation
Electrochemical lithium deintercalation was carried out
using electrodes made of the powder. The LiCoO2
powder (85%, w/w), a poly(vinylidene Ñuoride) binder
(10%, w/w) and acetylene black (5%, w/w) were well
mixed using 1-methyl-2-pyrrolidinone as a solvent to
make a viscous slurry. The slurry was spread in a 100
km thick layer on a stainless-steel substrate and then
dried overnight at 100 ¡C. The resulting composite was
covered with stainless-steel mesh as a current collector
and served as a test electrode. Prior to measurements,
the electrode was dried under vacuum at 100 ¡C for
more than 24 h.
A conventional three-electrode cell was used for elec-
trochemical measurements. The counter and reference
electrodes were lithium metal. The electrolyte solution
was a mixture of propylene carbonate (PC) and dime-
thoxyethane (DME) (1 : 1, v/v) containing 1 M LiClO4
(Mitsubishi Chemical). Electrochemical lithium deinter-
calation, which corresponds to charging in practical
batteries, was carried out at a constant current in an
argon-Ðlled glove-box. The current was chosen so that a
change of *x \ 1 in
takes 72 h (72 h rate).
Li1~xCoO2
The value of x was calculated through elapsed time,
current and the weight of
under the assump-
LiCoO2
tion that all the current was used in the deintercalation
reaction. After the
electrode had been charged
LiCoO2
to di†erent x values it was washed with DME and dried
under vacuum. The resulting electrode was covered with
polyethylene Ðlm and characterized by XRD and
Raman spectroscopy.
Spectroscopic measurements
The Raman spectra were recorded using a triple mono-
chromator (Jobin-Yvon, T-64000) with subtractive dis-
persion of the Ðrst two stages and a multichannel
charge-coupled device (CCD) detector. Excitation of all
the samples was carried out with 514.5 nm radiation (50
mW) from an argon ion laser (NEC, GLG3260). Input
polarization of the exciting radiation was vertical with
respect to the ground. The scattered light was collected
in
a
backscattering
(180¡)
geometry.
Unpolarized
Raman spectra of powdered samples and electrodes
after lithium deintercalation were measured using the
conventional technique.
To record polarized spectra, the thin-Ðlm sample was
placed so that the incident light was directed normal to
the surface of the Ðlm surface. The incident laser beam
was passed through a polarization rotator to obtain the
di†erent polarization geometries. The scattered light
was collected in the backscattering geometry, and was
analyzed using a polaroid plate and a j/4 plate. The
spectrometer detection sensitivities of the two polariza-
tion directions were not corrected. All measurements
were carried out at room temperature.
RESULTS AND DISCUSSION

powder and thin Ðlm are shown in Fig. 1. Both unpo-
larized spectra [(a) and (b)] exhibited two Raman bands

Raman spectral changes during lithium deintercalation
and their correlation with structural changes
Figure 4 shows the variation of the Raman spectra at
di†erent x values; AÈJ correspond to those in Fig. 3. In
the two-phase region (spectra BÈF in Fig. 4), the inten-
sity of each Raman band decreased, and a shoulder
peak appeared and grew in intensity at the low-
wavenumber side of each original band with increase in
x. The peak wavenumber and linewidth [full width at
half maximum (FWHM)] were determined by Lorentz-
ian lineshape analysis, and their variations are shown in
Fig. 5. The shoulder peaks of the
were not well separated because of their low intensity;
hence their values, which are designated by symbols in
parentheses in Fig. 5, are somewhat ambiguous. As
shown in Fig. 5(a), the peak wavenumbers of both the
original and the new lines remained almost unchanged

corresponds to those in Fig. 3.

FWHMs of the upper (closed circles) and lower (open circles)
in the two-phase region (0.04 \ x \ 0.25). Since the
space group of the second hexagonal phase is the same
as that of the Ðrst phase,2,4 the new two bands are also
unchanged in the whole two-phase region, the composi-
tions of the two phases should have remained constant.

The intensities of the two bands of the second hexago-
nal phase were much weaker than those of the Ðrst
semiconductor (10~3 S cm~1 at room temperature), and
its electrical conductivity increases with the removal of
lithium ions to become metallic at x P 0.20 (ca. 100 S
reduces the optical skin depth of the incident laser
beam, resulting in a decrease in Raman scattering inten-
sity.6 Hence the signiÐcant decrease in Raman intensity
can be qualitatively explained by the di†erence in elec-
trical conductivity between the Ðrst and second hexago-
As is seen in Fig. 5(a), the peak wavenumber of the
bands of the second hexagonal phase (584
and 480 cm~1, respectively) shifted downwards from
those of the corresponding bands of the Ðrst hexagonal
phase (596 and 486 cm~1, respectively). It was reported
that the Ðrst and second hexagonal phases have hexago-
nal lattice parameters of a \ 2.82
respectively.4 The expansion in
the c-axis direction of the second hexagonal phase has
been attributed to an increase in electrostatic repulsion
charged oxygenÈoxygen interactions increase with the
removal of the lithium ion.5 The observed downward
shifts of both bands are therefore attributed to such an
expansion in the c-axis direction.
It should be noted that the linewidths of both bands
of the second hexagonal phase were broader than those
( 1997 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
RAMAN STUDY OF LAYERED ROCK-SALT LiCoO2
of the Ðrst hexagonal phase, as shown in Fig. 5(b). This
implies that some kind of disordering was present in the
crystal structure of the second hexagonal phase. Since
only the lithium ions were removed by the reaction, it is
reasonable to consider that the lithium ions remaining
in the lattice randomly occupied the available sites on
the lithium planes in the layered rock-salt structure, and
this disordering led to the observed line broadening.
Such information about lithium sites in the lattice
cannot be obtained by XRD measurements since the
atomic scattering factor of lithium is much smaller than
In the region 0.25 \ x \ 0.47 (spectra G and H in
Fig. 4), the peak wavenumber of each band shifted grad-
ually in the lower direction. This feature is also in agree-
measurements,2,4,5 which showed that the c-axis length
increases with the removal of lithium ions.
At x \ 0.47, the phase transition from the second
hexagonal phase to a monoclinic phase was observed.
The space group of the monoclinic phase has been
with Z \ 2.2,4 The irreducible
representation for the total vibrational modes are 2Ag
active. Speculated atomic displacements of the 2Ag ] Bg
Raman-active modes are shown in Fig. 2(b). Corre-
lations between the vibrational modes in the hexagonal
and the monoclinic phase are shown in the Table 1. As
shown in Table 1, monoclinic distortion should cause
bands in C2/m; however, such peak splitting of
mode was not observed (spectrum I). Since
monoclinic distortion is not so severe in this material,4
the peak splitting would be small. In addition, the peak
intensity was extremely low and linewidth was broad.
Hence distinct splitting of the
Table 1. Correlation between the vibra-
observed. Dahn and co-workers2,3 suggested that the
monoclinic distortion is caused by lithium-ion ordering
at about x \ 0.5; however, no additional Raman bands
or line sharpening indicative of such ordering were
observed. This indicates that the lithium ions randomly
occupy the available lithium sites even in the lattice of
At x \ 0.56, the monoclinic phase was again trans-
formed into the hexagonal phase, and a single-phase
reaction of the hexagonal phase took place at x [ 0.56.
In spectrum J in this region, while the peak wavenum-
bers further shifted downwards, the linewidths became
slightly narrower. The latter fact conÐrms that line
broadening of the second hexagonal phase is caused by
because the degree of disordering should be maximum
exhibited two Raman bands at 486 and 596 cm~1,
tively, from the polarized Raman spectra of a c-axis
oriented thin Ðlm. Raman spectral changes during elec-
trochemical lithium intercalation were measured using
electrodes made of the powder. The observed spectral
changes were well correlated with the structural changes
namely as a series of phase transitions, that
have been proposed by other researchers although dis-
tinct peak splitting by the distortion in the monoclinic
phase was not observed. Line broadening of the Raman
bands of the second hexagonal phase and also those of
the monoclinic phase indicates that the lithium ions
remaining in the lattice randomly occupy the available
sites on the lithium planes, even in the lattice of the
monoclinic phase. The results obtained in this study
showed that Raman spectroscopy is a valuable tool for
studying the structural changes of cathode materials for
rechargeable lithium batteries during charging and dis-

Education, Science and Culture, Japan.
